div.div:
Figure 8. Evaluation map for state-centric serving
Long service requires measuring more than perplexity or window length: memory, tool-use, compaction, and recovery all matter

MCP-Atlas / tool
benchmarks

orchestration

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 382px; height: 1px; padding-top: 476px; margin-left: 472px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #0F172A;
display: inline-block; font-size: 16px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#0F172A, #d3daea); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div:
What current benchmarks mostly
cover
1. Recall from long histories
2. Multi-session temporal reasoning
3. Knowledge updates and contradictions
4. Tool-use planning in realistic
workflows

rect.style: fill: light-dark(rgb(255, 255, 255), rgb(18, 18, 18)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(203, 213, 225), rgb(47, 56, 66));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 402px; height: 1px; padding-top: 476px; margin-left: 982px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #0F172A;
display: inline-block; font-size: 16px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#0F172A, #d3daea); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div:
What a state-serving benchmark
should add
1. State carry-over rate after compaction
2. Retrieval-trigger precision / recall
3. Memory write amplification and token
overhead
4. Error-recovery half-life after tool
failure
5. Cross-session identity continuity under
new evidence
6. Privacy and policy isolation of portable
state

div.div: A state-centric system should be judged by what survives the next service window, not only by what fits inside the current one.
